title: Minimizing Blast Radius of a Leaked DB Credential
description:
## Shrink the Window of a Leaked Credential

A service connects to PostgreSQL using a username/password stored in a Kubernetes Secret. In a recent incident, that Secret was exfiltrated and the attacker had valid database access for days before anyone noticed.

You are redesigning credential handling specifically to **minimize the blast radius and useful lifetime of any single leaked database credential**.

Which approach best meets that goal?

options.text: Use a secrets engine that issues dynamic secrets: Vault's database engine generates a unique, short-lived DB user/password on demand with a lease/TTL, and automatically revokes it when the lease expires — so a leaked credential is valid only for a short window and is scoped to that one consumer.

Keep the single shared static credential but enable envelope encryption so the Secret is encrypted at rest with a KMS-backed data key; encryption at rest means a leaked credential can no longer be used by an attacker.
Keep the static credential but rotate it manually once per quarter and distribute the new value through a change-management ticket, ensuring it is never the same for more than 90 days.

Store the static credential in a secrets manager and grant every service read access to it, so credentials are centralized; centralization alone reduces blast radius because there is now only one place to revoke.

explanation: Blast radius and useful lifetime are minimized by making credentials short-lived and per-consumer. A dynamic-secrets engine (e.g. HashiCorp Vault's database secrets engine) mints a unique DB user with a TTL/lease at request time and revokes it automatically when the lease ends or is explicitly revoked. A leaked dynamic credential is therefore useful only for minutes/hours, is attributable to one consumer, and can be revoked instantly — exactly the goal. (b) misunderstands the threat: envelope encryption (a KMS master key wrapping a data key that encrypts the secret) protects the secret at rest, but the incident is about a credential that was exfiltrated and then used; once decrypted/in use it is just as valid for an attacker, and encryption at rest does nothing to shorten its lifetime. (c) still uses one long-lived shared credential valid for up to 90 days — far too long, shared across all instances, and a manual process that's slow to revoke. (d) centralizes storage but the credential is still static, long-lived, and shared by every service, so a single leak still grants broad, lasting access; centralization helps management but not the lifetime/blast-radius of the credential itself.

hints.title: Short-lived beats well-stored
hints.body: The smallest blast radius comes from credentials that expire quickly and are unique per consumer, not from better-encrypting a long-lived shared secret.

hints.title: Dynamic vs static
hints.body: Dynamic secrets are generated on demand with a lease/TTL and auto-revoked. Static secrets persist until someone rotates them.
